macro_segments.title: The status of the egg in mixtures and the principle of dabar she'yesh lo matirin

micro_segments.title: The Gemara's challenge: why isn't the egg batel if the problem is rabbinic or related to trefah
Rav Pappa's resolution: the brayta author holds that dabar she'be'minyan (counted items) never become batel
The brayta of pressed figs (litra ketzi'ot): counted items are not batel even for rabbinic issues
The debate between Rabbi Eliezer and Rabbi Yehoshua on whether to count the tops or entire barrels of figs
Rav Ashi's alternative: the egg problem is purely Yom Tov related, and dabar she'yesh lo matirin applies even to doubts in rabbinic matters

macro_segments.title: The egg and its mother: the brayta stating eggs and hens are both permitted

micro_segments.title: The brayta of Rabbi Eliezer: egg and mother may be eaten (interpreted as exaggeration for emphasis)
The case of a hen purchased without specified purpose (kegon she'lakcha stam) and retroactive determination via breira
Rav Mari: the brayta employs guzma (embellishment) rather than literal teaching about eating shells

macro_segments.title: Eggs born on Shabbat and permissibility on Yom Tov: the debate of Rav and Rabbi Yochanan

micro_segments.title: Itmar: whether an egg born on Shabbat becomes permitted on Yom Tov; Rav says forbidden, Rabbi Yochanan says permitted
The Gemara's analysis: the debate concerns whether Shabbat and Yom Tov are one kedusha or two kedushos
Resolution: the debate centers on hachana de-Rabbah; Rav holds it applies, Rabbi Yochanan does not
Story of Rav Adda bar Ahava: eggs born Yom Tov to Shabbat, and the issue of cooking for the next day
Story of Rav Pappa: eggs born Shabbat to Yom Tov; Rav declines to rule when drunk, rules next day in favor of Rav Yochanan
Rava's ruling: the halacha follows Rav in three matters, including eggs born on one day and eaten on the next

macro_segments.title: Wood branches falling into an oven on Yom Tov and the limits of dabar she'yesh lo matirin

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yochanan: wood that falls from a tree on Shabbat cannot be used on Yom Tov, unlike eggs
The distinction: eggs are permitted tomorrow because they had potential use today; branches had no use on Shabbat, creating false inference
Rav Masna: if wood falls into an oven, add extra wood and make it batel (majority rule)
Gemara challenge: how can you create bitel of muktzah proactively if dabar she'yesh lo matirin prevents bitel?
Resolution: bitel works here because the wood is in the process of being burned and loses its identity

topical_tags.term: dabar she'yesh lo matirin (something that will become permitted)
Beis Shammai vs. Beis Hillel (egg dispute)
Rabbi Eliezer vs. Rabbi Yehoshua (pressed figs debate)
Rav vs. Rabbi Yochanan (Shabbat-Yom Tov egg)
